title: Lesson 001 – Talking About Uncertainty
grammarFocus: penso che / credo che / mi sembra che / pare che / ho l'impressione che + present subjunctive
objective: Learn to express uncertainty, impressions, opinions, and doubt using natural Italian.

sentences.italian: Penso che sia una buona idea.
sentences.literal: I think that it be a good idea.
sentences.natural: I think it's a good idea.
sentences.note: After 'penso che', Italian often uses the subjunctive: 'sia' is the subjunctive of essere; the plain indicative would be 'è'.

sentences.italian: Non credo che sia così semplice.
sentences.literal: I don't believe that it be so simple.
sentences.natural: I don't think it's that simple.
sentences.note: Negative opinion phrases often trigger the subjunctive. Here 'sia' is the subjunctive of essere; the plain indicative would be 'è'.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra che tu abbia ragione.
sentences.literal: It seems to me that you have-subjunctive reason.
sentences.natural: It seems to me that you're right.
sentences.note: 'tu abbia' is the subjunctive of avere; the plain indicative would be 'tu hai'. Italian says 'you have reason' for 'you're right'.

sentences.italian: Ho l'impressione che stiano aspettando noi.
sentences.literal: I have the impression that they be waiting for us.
sentences.natural: I get the impression they're waiting for us.
sentences.note: 'stiano' is the subjunctive of stare; the plain indicative would be 'stanno' (and 'stia' → 'sta').

sentences.italian: Pare che il treno sia in ritardo.
sentences.literal: It seems that the train be late.
sentences.natural: Apparently, the train is late.
sentences.note: 'Pare che' often introduces information that feels uncertain or reported. Here 'sia' is the subjunctive of essere; the plain indicative would be 'è'.

sentences.italian: Dubito che funzioni davvero.
sentences.literal: I doubt that it work really.
sentences.natural: I doubt it really works.
sentences.note: 'Dubito che' strongly triggers the subjunctive. 'funzioni' is the subjunctive of funzionare; the plain indicative would be 'funziona'.

sentences.italian: È possibile che abbiano già deciso.
sentences.literal: It is possible that they have-subjunctive already decided.
sentences.natural: It's possible they've already decided.
sentences.note: 'È possibile che' takes the subjunctive because the idea is uncertain. 'abbiano' is the subjunctive of avere (loro); the plain indicative would be 'hanno'.

sentences.italian: Non sono sicuro che venga anche lui.
sentences.literal: I am not sure that he come also him.
sentences.natural: I'm not sure he's coming too.
sentences.note: 'venga' is the subjunctive of venire; the plain indicative would be 'viene'.

sentences.italian: Mi sa che abbiamo sbagliato strada.
sentences.literal: To me it knows that we have taken the wrong road.
sentences.natural: I think we've gone the wrong way.
sentences.note: 'Mi sa che' is a very common spoken phrase and normally takes the indicative — here 'abbiamo' is the plain indicative of avere (no subjunctive needed after 'mi sa che').

sentences.italian: Può darsi che abbiano capito male.
sentences.literal: It can give itself that they have-subjunctive understood badly.
sentences.natural: Maybe they misunderstood.
sentences.note: 'Può darsi che' is a very useful uncertainty phrase and takes the subjunctive. 'abbiano' is the subjunctive of avere (loro); the plain indicative would be 'hanno'.

sentences.italian: Credo che faccia troppo freddo per uscire.
sentences.literal: I believe that it make too much cold to go out.
sentences.natural: I think it's too cold to go out.
sentences.note: 'faccia' is the subjunctive of fare; the plain indicative would be 'fa' (Italian says 'fa freddo', literally 'it makes cold', for 'it's cold').

sentences.italian: Spero che tu possa venire alla festa.
sentences.literal: I hope that you can-subjunctive come to the party.
sentences.natural: I hope you can come to the party.
sentences.note: 'Spero che' (I hope) takes the subjunctive. 'tu possa' is the subjunctive of potere; the plain indicative would be 'tu puoi'.

sentences.italian: Temo che non ci sia più tempo.
sentences.literal: I fear that there be no more time.
sentences.natural: I'm afraid there's no time left.
sentences.note: 'Temo che' (I fear) triggers the subjunctive. 'ci sia' is the subjunctive of essere; the plain indicative 'there is' would be 'c'è'.

sentences.italian: È probabile che arrivino in ritardo.
sentences.literal: It is probable that they arrive-subjunctive late.
sentences.natural: They'll probably arrive late.
sentences.note: 'È probabile che' expresses likelihood, so it takes the subjunctive 'arrivino'; the plain indicative would be 'arrivano'.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra che lui non dica la verità.
sentences.literal: It seems to me that he not say-subjunctive the truth.
sentences.natural: It seems to me he's not telling the truth.
sentences.note: 'dica' is the subjunctive of dire; the plain indicative would be 'dice'.

sentences.italian: Pare che la riunione vada avanti senza di noi.
sentences.literal: It seems that the meeting go-subjunctive ahead without of us.
sentences.natural: Apparently the meeting is going ahead without us.
sentences.note: 'vada' is the subjunctive of andare; the plain indicative would be 'va'.

sentences.italian: Non sono sicuro che riesca a finire in tempo.
sentences.literal: I am not sure that he succeed-subjunctive to finish in time.
sentences.natural: I'm not sure he'll manage to finish in time.
sentences.note: 'riesca' is the subjunctive of riuscire (to manage / succeed); the plain indicative would be 'riesce'.

sentences.italian: Credo che non capisca bene l'italiano.
sentences.literal: I believe that he not understand-subjunctive well the Italian.
sentences.natural: I don't think he understands Italian well.
sentences.note: 'capisca' is the subjunctive of capire; the plain indicative would be 'capisce'. Italian often keeps the doubt on the 'credo che' clause.

sentences.italian: Dubito che lui sappia cosa sta succedendo.
sentences.literal: I doubt that he know-subjunctive what is happening.
sentences.natural: I doubt he knows what's going on.
sentences.note: 'sappia' is the subjunctive of sapere; the plain indicative would be 'sa'.

sentences.italian: Può darsi che vogliano cambiare programma.
sentences.literal: It can give itself that they want-subjunctive to change plan.
sentences.natural: Maybe they want to change the plan.
sentences.note: 'vogliano' is the subjunctive of volere (loro); the plain indicative would be 'vogliono' (and 'voglia' → 'vuole').

patternDrill.literal: I think that it be better to wait.
patternDrill.natural: I think it's better to wait.
patternDrill.note: 'sia' is the subjunctive of essere; the plain indicative would be 'è'.

patternDrill.literal: I believe that it have sense.
patternDrill.note: 'abbia' is the subjunctive of avere; the plain indicative would be 'ha'.

patternDrill.literal: It seems to me that something be missing.
patternDrill.natural: It seems like something is missing.
patternDrill.note: 'manchi' is the subjunctive of mancare; the plain indicative would be 'manca'.

patternDrill.literal: It seems that they have-subjunctive changed idea.
patternDrill.note: 'abbiano' is the subjunctive of avere (loro); the plain indicative would be 'hanno'.

patternDrill.literal: I don't believe that it work well.
patternDrill.natural: I don't think it works well.
patternDrill.note: 'funzioni' is the subjunctive of funzionare; the plain indicative would be 'funziona'.

activeRecall.prompt: I think it's a good idea.
activeRecall.answer: Penso che sia una buona idea.

activeRecall.prompt: I don't think it's that simple.
activeRecall.answer: Non credo che sia così semplice.

activeRecall.prompt: It seems to me that you're right.
activeRecall.answer: Mi sembra che tu abbia ragione.
Pare che il treno sia in ritardo.
Può darsi che abbiano capito male.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Pensi che sia una buona idea?
conversationBuilder.natural: Do you think it's a good idea?

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Non lo so. Non credo che sia così semplice.
conversationBuilder.natural: I don't know. I don't think it's that simple.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Mi sembra che tu abbia qualche dubbio.
conversationBuilder.natural: It seems to me that you have some doubts.

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Sì, ho l'impressione che manchi qualcosa.
conversationBuilder.natural: Yes, I get the impression something is missing.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Può darsi che tu abbia ragione.

grammarExplanation.intro: In Italian, when you express uncertainty, opinion, doubt, possibility, or impression, you often use the subjunctive after "che".

comparison.label: Subjunctive
comparison.italian: Penso che sia una buona idea.
comparison.english: I think it is a good idea.

grammarExplanation.explanation: The idea is no longer a plain fact; it's filtered through opinion, doubt, impression, or uncertainty.
grammarExplanation.note: Native speakers sometimes use the indicative in casual speech (especially after penso che / credo che), but the subjunctive is safer, more educated, and very useful at B1/B2.

masteryChecklist: Recognise the subjunctive forms sia, abbia, faccia, vada, dica, possa, sappia, voglia, riesca, capisca.
Translate the 5 active-recall prompts into Italian.
Create 3 new sentences using penso che, mi sembra che, può darsi che.
Explain why Italian uses the subjunctive after uncertainty phrases.
